author: [Shahryar Mohsenin,Kurt P Munz]
title: How Perceptual Disfluency Affects Consumer Choices

kids.content: How Perceptual Disfluency Affects Consumer Choices
SHAHRYAR MOHSENIN KURT P. MUNZ

Marketing materials often create difficulties for consumers through elements like unconventional fonts or distracting background images, leading to perceptual disfluency—a sense of difficulty encoding information. During choices, consumers can mistake perceptual disfluency for choice difficulty, a feeling of indecision. Building on this idea, 11 preregistered experiments (N 9,042) show that when making choices, perceptual disfluency can lead consumers to rely on information that feels more intuitively appealing to them, such as a familiar brand, a preferred country of origin, or a recommendation, rather than on information they must carefully consider like numerical product specifications. Differing from the effect of perceptual disfluency in other situations, during choices, this effect occurs because consumers process information less deeply, engaging in relatively more intuitive and less analytical processing. This effect is amplified when consumers endorse a “fast-is-accurate” lay theory. In contrast, it does not occur when consumers do not mistake perceptual disfluency for choice difficulty, as when prompted to consider the actual reason for the disfluency, or when not making a choice.
Keywords: perceptual disfluency, brand, recommendation, decision-making, choice difficulty

resolution. These elements contribute to perceptual disfluency—feelings of difficulty experienced when encoding information (Alter and Oppenheimer 2009). When evaluating products, perceptual disfluency can have a negative impact; consumers can misattribute the negative feelings caused by, for example, a disfluent font in a retailer’s flyer as their feelings about the product they are evaluating (Schwarz et al. 1991). Although prior work has demonstrated this metacognitive process for evaluations, the effect of perceptual disfluency on consumer choices between options and the underlying mechanism remains less well understood, despite its critical importance for marketing managers.

For some tasks, like those that involve logical reasoning, perceptual disfluency can serve as a cue that something is amiss, increasing vigilance and motivating deeper processing than otherwise (Alter et al. 2007; Meyer et al. 2015; Song and Schwarz 2008). When extrapolated to consumer choices, such a process could shift relative preferences toward options that appear more analytical—relying less on intuitively appealing and easily understood cues, like brands, and more on information that comes to be

However, the impact of disfluency depends on the inferences consumers draw from it (Schwarz 2004), and when making consumer choices, the inferences drawn from perceptual disfluency may differ from other settings (Whittlesea 1993). For instance, when making choices, consumers may mistake perceptual disfluency for choice difficulty—a feeling of indecision experienced when contemplating trade-offs between options (Novemsky et al. 2007). As a result, it can lead them to defer choosing among the options until later or choose a compromise option to avoid making a seemingly difficult trade-off (Novemsky et al. 2007).

Building on this earlier work, we highlight how experiencing perceptual disfluency in a consumer choice setting lends itself to an inference of choice difficulty (Novemsky et al. 2007), and we examine a novel way it can influence decision-making, meaningfully advancing on the past work. We propose that, in addition to deferring choices or choosing compromise options, consumers who, due to perceptual disfluency, feel unsure about which option to select will also exhibit a tendency to shift their relative preferences toward options that feel more intuitively appealingthose with a more familiar brand, a trusted country of origin, or those that are recommended. Noticing how this effect seems opposite to what might happen if perceptual disfluency would lead consumers to choose options that appear more analytical (Alter et al. 2007), we also probe the underlying psychological mechanism. Specifically, we investigate whether perceptual disfluency experienced during decision-making leads consumers to process information less analytically and more intuitively than they would otherwise, leading them to focus on product information that is salient and easy to understand without careful attention and scrutiny.

These results highlight how the effect of perceptual disfluency depends on how consumers interpret it (Schwarz 2004; Thompson and Ince 2013). When consumers mistake perceptual disfluency for indecision, we show how its effect differs from what has been documented previously in the literature (Alter et al. 2007; Meyer et al. 2015), highlighting the critical role that making consumer choices plays in this process. The results also offer actionable insights and implications for marketers and brand managers. For example, the importance of brands or recommendations may increase in retail settings which trigger perceptual disfluency.
THEORETICAL DEVELOPMENT Perceptual Disfluency and Evaluations
Disfluency refers to the subjective experience of difficulty encountered during cognitive tasks such as decision-

making (Alter and Oppenheimer 2009; Novemsky et al. 2007; Schwarz 2004). It can manifest in various forms, including perceptual, conceptual, and retrieval disfluency (Alter and Oppenheimer 2009; Schwarz et al. 2021). Perceptual disfluency, the focus of this research, refers to difficulty experienced when encoding or processing the perceptual features of a stimulus (Alter and Oppenheimer

list items.content: 2009; Graf, Mayer, and Landwehr 2018; Janiszewski and Meyvis 2001). This type of disfluency is commonly observed in consumer settings—for example, when reading a retail flyer—in which factors such as font (Alter et al. 2007; Lee and Labroo 2004; Novemsky et al. 2007; Sela and Berger 2012; Simmons and Nelson 2006; Song and Schwarz 2008), figure-ground contrast (Reber, Winkielman, and Schwarz 1998; Thompson and Ince 2013), and visual clarity (Reber and Schwarz 1999; Whittlesea, Jacoby, and Girard 1990) may contribute to how easy or difficult information can be to process.

kids.content: A substantial body of research has documented a negative impact of perceptual disfluency on judgments and evaluations in a wide range of contexts (Alter and Oppenheimer 2009; Schwarz et al. 2021). These can include evaluations of products (King and Janiszewsk 2011; Mohsenin and Munz 2024), services (Thompson and Ince 2013), brands (Labroo, Dhar, and Schwarz 2008; Lee and Labroo 2004; Lee and Shavitt 2009; Smith and Abell 2025), and perceptions of price (Hodges and Chen 2022; Thomas and Morwitz 2009).

Existing research suggests that disfluency influences evaluations through a process of metacognitive attribution; individuals experience a feeling of difficulty when processing information and make inferences about the cause of that negative feeling (Schwarz 2004). If a task proves more difficult than expected, people may mistakenly attribute their feeling as being about whatever they are evaluating (Schwarz et al. 1991; Whittlesea and Williams 2001). For example, when product information is presented in a hard-to-read font, consumers may misattribute the unpleasantness of the experience to the product rather than to the font, resulting in less favorable evaluations (Alter and Oppenheimer 2009).

Evidence to support this metacognitive mechanism comes from attributional discounting effects, which demonstrate that when people identify that the perceptual disfluency is unrelated to the target they are evaluating, they stop using it as a cue for making judgments (Alter and Oppenheimer 2009; Schwarz 2004). That is, when people are explicitly warned that the difficulty is caused by something irrelevant (e.g., poor font choice), the negative effects on their evaluations are reduced or even eliminated (Fang, Singh, and Ahluwalia 2007; Novemsky et al. 2007; Pocheptsova, Labroo, and Dhar

list items.content: 2010; Schwarz et al. 1991; Sela and Berger 2012; Walter et al. 2020).

et al. 2010; Schwarz 2004). That is, if a certain task or context leads people to interpret the meaning of their disfluency experience differently, they may also make different judgments or decisions as a result. Consistent with this idea, prior research shows that perceptual disfluency does not always lead to negative evaluations. Rather, in certain evaluative contexts, disfluency can signal positive attributes such as novelty (Sung, Vanman, and Hartley 2022), innovativeness (Cho and Schwarz 2006), competence (Thompson and Ince 2013), or uniqueness (Pocheptsova et al. 2010).

Further supporting the idea that perceptual disfluency can lead to analytical information processing, Song and Schwarz (2008) found that perceptual disfluency attenuated the Moses illusion. When participants were asked, “How many animals of each kind did Moses take aboard the ark?” 88% incorrectly answered “two,” failing to notice that the question was not about Noah’s ark. Presenting the question in a disfluent font reduced this error rate to 53%. Similarly, Diemand-Yauman, Oppenheimer, and Vaughan (2011) showed that difficult-to-read fonts improved memory recall by 14%, suggesting deeper attention, and Simmons and Nelson (2006) found that disfluent presentation led gamblers to more often bet against the favorite, suggesting deeper consideration of the point spread.

Although prior research has shown how perceptual disfluency affects judgment and evaluation, its influence on choices between options in consumer settings remains less well understood. To address this, we next review existing findings on the effects of perceptual disfluency across various domains, from solving logical puzzles to making product choices.

If this shift to more analytical processing also occurs in consumer decision-making settings, perceptual disfluency could shift preferences away from intuitively appealing cues, such as brand names, and toward information requiring careful scrutiny, such as numerical product specifications (Chaiken and Ledgerwood 2012). In line with this idea, Alter et al. (2007) found that when product review information was presented in a disfluent font, participants’ favorability ratings were influenced more by argument quality than by communicator attractiveness. Perceptual disfluency has also been shown to promote variety seeking (Huang and Kwong 2016; Walter et al. 2020), leading consumers to consider unfamiliar products (Walter et al. 2020), suggesting lower reliance on familiar product cues like brands.
Perceptual Disfluency and Choices

When solving mathematical and logical puzzles, Alter et al. (2007) provided evidence that perceptual disfluency improved participants’ performance. For both the cognitive reflection test1 (CRT; Frederick 2005) and a syllogistic reasoning task,2 participants exposed to perceptually disfluent fonts answered a greater proportion of questions correctly than those exposed to perceptually fluent fonts. The authors reasoned that the research participants interpreted the perceptual disfluency due to the fonts as a metacognitive cue that something was amiss or suspicious. In turn, this prompted them to think more critically and analytically, allowing them to overcome their initial intuitions and provide more correct answers.

However, the link between perceptual disfluency and enhanced analytical reasoning has been questioned. Meyer et al. (2015) found no evidence across 16 experiments that disfluent fonts improved CRT or syllogistic task performance. Similarly, Thompson et al. (2013) found no relationship between perceptual disfluency and analytical processing. Others have also recently questioned this link (Rummer, Schweppe, and Schwede 2016; Sirota, Theodoropoulou, and Juanchich 2021). These inconsistencies suggest that perceptual disfluency does not uniformly activate analytical processing. Indeed, as we discuss next, the effects may depend critically on the nature of the task.

These results were interpreted as relating to an analytical cognitive processing style. Although a variety of labels have been proposed (Chaiken and Trope 1999), information processing is typically conceived as occurring through two modes: intuitive processing (also called heuristic, peripheral, or system I) and analytical processing (also called systematic, central, or system II; Chaiken and Ledgerwood 2012; Kahneman and Frederick 2002; Petty and Cacioppo 1986). Intuitive processing is less effortful, involving minimal consideration of information and leading individuals to rely on salient and easily comprehended cues when making judgments (Chaiken and Ledgerwood 2012). In contrast, analytical processing demands significant cognitive effort, requiring careful attention, deep thinking, intensive reasoning, and thorough scrutiny of available information (Maheswaran, Mackie, and Chaiken 1992).
Task-Dependent Influence of Perceptual Disfluency on Consumer Choices

As stated previously, metacognitive experiences are highly context dependent (Alter and Oppenheimer 2009) because individuals rely on naive theories to interpret their feelings (Schwarz 2004). Put simply, when a particular task or context causes people to interpret the meaning of their disfluency experience differently, it can also lead to a different influence on the judgments or decisions they make.

kids.content: Importantly, in a consumer decision-making context, some researchers have proposed that consumers can make a different inference from perceptual disfluency than discussed

earlier. Specifically, instead of interpreting it as a sign that something is amiss (Alter et al. 2007), when making choices, consumers may mistake perceptual disfluency for choice difficulty—a feeling of indecision experienced when contemplating trade-offs between options (Novemsky et al. 2007). This can lead them to defer making a choice or to choose a compromise option (Novemsky et al. 2007). The results suggest that consumers try to avoid making trade-offs when they experience perceptual disfluency. However, it is less clear how perceptual disfluency may affect consumers’ choices when trade-offs cannot be avoided, as when choosing between two options without the option to defer and there is no possibility of compromise.

In this situation, we propose that if consumers misinterpret perceptual disfluency as choice difficulty, it may have consequences that extend further than previously appreciated. Specifically, in addition to deferral and compromise (Novemsky et al. 2007), it seems possible that consumers might react to feeling unsure about what to choose by relying on information they do feel sure about (Hoch and Ha 1986; Lynch Jr. and Srull 1982; Rucker et al. 2014). In other words, they may rely on information that feels more familiar to them, based on their own past experience (Whittlesea 1993; Lee 2001), rather than rely on information they have only just encountered in the choice context and must come to appreciate through careful attention and scrutiny, like numerical product specifications. As a result, they may be more likely to choose options that are offered by a familiar brand, are from a preferred country of origin, or are recommended by a familiar source.

This potential impact of perceptual disfluency on consumers’ choices may align with the theories of information processing described earlier (Chaiken and Trope 1999), but in the opposite way. Specifically, the information that we predict will become more influential when consumers feel unsure about what to choose, like familiar brands, tends to be intuitively appealing without careful analytical processing. Thus, it could indicate that consumers experiencing perceptual disfluency when choosing between products will use a more intuitive and less analytical information processing style than otherwise. Indeed, researchers have previously identified each of these specific types of information as being “easily noticed and easily understood cues” that consumers tend to rely on when they process information intuitively (Chaiken and Ledgerwood 2012, 247). This is true for brands (Dawar and Parker 1994; Gunasti and Ross Jr. 2010; Janiszewski and Meyvis 2001; Maheswaran et al. 1992), countries of origin (Chang 2004; Hong and Wyer Jr. 1989; Maheswaran 1994), and recommendations (Forehand, Gastil, and Smith 2004).
CURRENT RESEARCH
Perceptual disfluency during decision-making can lead consumers to misinterpret the experience as choice

difficulty or indecision (Novemsky et al. 2007). We propose that this misattribution prompts consumers to rely more on information that feels familiar to them based on past experiences—such as known brands, preferred countries of origin, or trusted recommendations—rather than on newly encountered information only appreciable through more careful scrutiny. We also examine whether this shift reflects a change in information processing style. Specifically, we investigate whether perceptual disfluency leads consumers to process information less analytically and more intuitively.

Eleven large-scale, preregistered experiments examine the effect of perceptual disfluency on consumer choices (table 1). Experiments 1A–1E demonstrate the effect of perceptual disfluency across different contexts, including hypothetical and incentivized choice settings and realistic marketplace environments. The experiments examine managerially relevant factors contributing to perceptual disfluency, including font, background image, image resolution, and device screen size. Experiment 2 examines the serially mediating role of choice difficulty and processing depth as the underlying mechanism. Experiment 3 provides theoretical support by demonstrating the moderating role of lay theories about decision speed. Experiments 4 and 5 investigate the role of consumers attributing perceptual disfluency to choice difficulty through moderation. Specifically, experiment 4 employs an attributional discounting manipulation to forewarn participants about the source of disfluency, whereas experiment 5 tests the role of choice difficulty by examining how consumers evaluate options when making a choice or not.

These findings are significant for two main reasons. First, they differ from previous research, which suggested that perceptual disfluency encourages more analytical processing (Alter et al. 2007) or has no effect (Meyer et al. 2015). In the context of choices between products, our results indicate that consumers rely less on analytic or numeric information and more on intuitive or familiar product information. We highlight the critical role that consumer decision-making plays in this process. Although the past research focused mostly on logical puzzles, in which perceptual disfluency could raise suspicion, we suggest that consumers making choices mistake perceptual disfluency for indecision. This, in turn, leads them to focus on intuitively appealing product information like familiar brands or trusted recommendations or countries of origin. Second, the findings have practical relevance. When elements in marketing materials reduce perceptual fluency, consumers tend to prioritize familiarity over analytical evaluation. This insight can inform retailers and designers aiming to influence consumer judgment.

EXPERIMENT 1A: CHOICE AND DEFERAL

How does perceptual disfluency affect consumer choices? In some settings, people may interpret perceptual disfluency with suspicion (Alter et al. 2007; Song and Schwarz 2008), leading them to more carefully analyze information. However, during decision-making, some past research suggests that consumers may mistake perceptual disfluency for choice difficulty or indecision, which can lead them to defer making a choice (Novemsky et al. 2007). Additionally, we predicted that consumers may turn to information that feels more certain when they feel unsure about what to choose (Hoch and Ha 1986; Lynch Jr. and Srull 1982; Rucker et al. 2014). Specifically, they may prioritize information that feels familiar based on prior experience (Lee 2001; Whittlesea 1993) rather than engage deeply with unfamiliar information such as technical specifications or numerical details.

Experiment 1A tested for this effect of perceptual disfluency on consumer choice behavior. We manipulated perceptual disfluency through fonts, and we presented participants with three options. One option had a familiar brand and favored country of origin but less-desirable technical specifications. Another option instead had an unfamiliar brand and less-preferred country of origin but preferable technical specifications. As a third option, participants could defer choice. We predicted that the choice share of the first option would increase when the options were presented in a perceptually disfluent versus fluent font. We additionally sought to replicate the effect of perceptual disfluency on deferral (Novemsky et al. 2007). This would provide suggestive evidence that both outcomes could be the result of choice difficulty.
Method

Following a preregistered plan for recruitment and data analysis, 602 American and Canadian participants (Mage ¼ 39.15 years, SD¼11.32; 48.8% female) from Amazon Mechanical Turk (MTurk) were paid a small sum for completing this experiment, and of them all were included in the analysis. As with many consumer purchases, participants could choose between the options or defer the choice (choose none). The stimuli for this experiment were two blenders. This study used a two-cell between-participants design manipulating perceptual disfluency. Following the existing literature (Alter et al. 2007; Lee and Labroo 2004; Novemsky et al. 2007; Sela and Berger 2012; Simmons and Nelson 2006; Song and Schwarz 2008), we manipulated perceptual disfluency by presenting the two options to half of the participants with the descriptions presented in
an easy-to-read font (Arial), whereas the others saw the same descriptions in a hard-to-read font (Amarix SC, cursive). Figure 1 depicts the stimuli and manipulation.

Participants were asked to imagine that they needed to buy a new blender to make smoothies and juices. One of the blenders was preferable based on technical specifications (i.e., it had higher wattage, more capacity, and blending functions), whereas the other blender featured a familiar brand name, a salient and easily understood cue (Maheswaran et al. 1992). Specifically, the options consisted of a familiar brand in the participants’ market (Hamilton beach) and an unfamiliar hypothetical foreign brand that was not available on the market (iTOChin). We selected these brands as a strong initial test of our hypothesis. That is, both brand (Dawar and Parker 1994; Gunasti and Ross Jr. 2010; Maheswaran et al. 1992) and country-of-origin (Chang 2004; Maheswaran 1994; Newman and Dhar 2014) can be easily understood. Because iTOChin suggests that the product may be Chinese, it might serve as a negative intuitive cue to some participants (Maheswaran 1994; Newman and Dhar 2014). Some information, such as price, products’ material, and weight of the options was kept constant between the two options. All participants saw both options presented in a top-and-bottom format on the same page. The presentation order of brands was counterbalanced.

The primary dependent variable was the response to the question, “If you were going to buy a blender, which one would you like to choose?” Participants could select one of three options: blender A, blender B, or “For now, I would not buy either one of these.” Near the end, we asked participants to answer two memory check questions, “Which blender has only 5 blending functions?” (Hamilton Beach or iTOChin), and “What was the wattage of Blender A/B [iTOChin]? (700w, or 900w).” For a manipulation check of perceptual disfluency, participants were asked to rate, “How difficult was it to read the information?” (1 ¼ not at all difficult, 7¼very difficult). For a manipulation check of brand familiarity, participants were asked to rate, “How familiar were you with the survey blender brands?” (1¼not at all familiar, 7 ¼extremely familiar; Lee and Shavitt 2009; Maheswaran et al. 1992).
Results

Manipulation Checks. Participants rated the information presented in Amarix SC, cursive font as more difficult to read (M ¼4.83, SD¼1.91) than the easy-to-read Arial font (M ¼1.78, SD¼1.16, t(600) ¼ 23.74, p < .001, d¼1.93). Additionally, a paired-sample t-test on brand familiarity revealed that participants were more familiar with Hamilton Beach (M ¼5.12, SD¼2.00) than iTOChin (M¼1.47, SD¼1.16, t(601) ¼ 39.48, p < .001, d¼1.61).

and scrutiny,” we conducted a separate test with an independent sample from the same population (N¼100). Participants (Mage ¼ 41.55 years, SD¼12.73; 44% female) in this pilot experiment rated each piece of product information presented in the main experiment based on how intuitive or analytical they perceived it to be. We first showed participants the stimuli used in the main experiment. Among participants, the stimuli were randomly presented in the easy- or hard-to-read fonts used in the main experiment. As a dependent measure, for each piece of information presented, we asked participants the extent to which the information “seems like an intuitive cue to you—something that feels salient and easy to understand when evaluating a product.” We next asked the extent to which the information “seems like an analytical cuesomething that requires more deliberate thought, careful attention, and detailed analysis when evaluating a product.” We computed a difference score between ratings of these two measures for each piece of product information.

On the computed difference score, as expected, there was no interaction with the perceptual disfluency manipulation (F(3, 96) ¼ 1.26, p ¼ .29). More importantly, as predicted, brand name was rated as significantly more intuitive and less analytical than the average of the other features (F(1, 98) ¼ 23.67, p < .001, ηp2 ¼ 0.19). It was also rated significantly more intuitive and less analytical than each of the attributes individually. Specifically, it was more intuitive and less analytical than the number of blending functions (F(1,98) ¼ 7.62, p ¼ .007, ηp2 ¼ 0.07), the
blender capacity (F(1,98) ¼ 17.78, p < .001, ηp2 ¼ 0.15), and its power/wattage (F(1,98) ¼ 35.89, p < .001, ηp2 ¼ 0.27). These findings provide evidence for our classification of the cues.

Choice Share. There was no significant brand order effect (p ¼ .083) or interaction of order with the font type (p ¼ .702) on choice share. Thus, as preregistered, we collapsed across counterbalancing orders. We tested for overall differences in choices first. To do so, we ran a chisquare test with perceptual disfluency (hard-to-read, easyto-read font) as the independent variable and choice share (Hamilton beach ¼1, iTOChin ¼2, and deferral ¼3) as the dependent variable, revealing significant differences (χ2(2) ¼ 23.42, p < .001, Cramer’s V ¼ 0.197; figure 2). As expected, most participants seeing an easy-to-read font chose the option with preferable specifications. However, when confronted with a hard-to-read font, participants were relatively more likely to choose the option with a familiar brand name and they were also more likely to defer their decision.

Next, we recoded the dependent variable to be binary (did the participant choose the familiar brand or not?) and analyzed the data with a chi-square test. This is the primary test of the prediction that reading choice options in a hardto-read font leads people choose a familiar brand more often. The results supported this prediction. Finally, we recoded the original dependent variable to be binary (did the participant defer or not?) and analyzed the data with a chi-square test. This is the primary test of the prediction that people are more likely to defer in the perceptually disfluent condition (Novemsky et al. 2007). The results supported this prediction. Whereas 6.6% of participants who received the easy-to-read font deferred, 15.3% of those who received the hard-to-read font did so (χ2(1) ¼ 11.70, p < .001, Cramer’s V¼0.139), replicating previously reported findings (Novemsky et al. 2007).

Memory Checks. One explanation for these results could be that when the information was perceptually disfluent, participants only read the brand names and did not read the details, an inattention account. To explore this possibility, we analyzed responses to the memory questions. Participants in the easy-to-read condition answered correctly more often than participants in the hard-to-read font condition for both items (blending function: Peasy-toread ¼ 89.4% vs. Phard-to-read ¼ 84% correct; χ2(1) ¼ 3.81, p ¼ .051; blender power in wattage: Peasy-to-read ¼ 91.7% vs. Phard-to-read ¼ 79.3% correct; χ2(1) ¼ 18.83, p < .001).

To examine if there is an effect of perceptual disfluency above and beyond inattention, we analyzed the responses of 76.4% of participants (N¼460) who responded correctly to both memory-check questions, reasoning that

participants must first read the information in order to remember it. Consumers chose the familiar brand more often when it was difficult to read the information (Peasy-toread ¼ 21.2%, N ¼53/250 vs. Phard-to-read ¼ 30.5%, N¼64/ 210; χ2(1) ¼ 5.18, p ¼ .023, Cramer’s V ¼ 0.106), and they were also more likely to defer choice (Peasy-to-read ¼ 6.4%, N¼16/250 vs. Phard-to-read ¼ 13.3%, N ¼28/210; χ2(1) ¼ 6.34, p ¼ .012, Cramer’s V ¼ 0.117). Thus, the same pattern of results occurs when controlling for inattention in this way.
Discussion

How does perceptual disfluency shape consumer choice? Prior work suggests that consumers may misattribute perceptual disfluency to difficulty deciding, leading them to defer choice (Novemsky et al. 2007). Building on this, we propose that perceptual disfluency may also prompt consumers to rely on information that feels more certain when they feel unsure about what to choose (Hoch and Ha 1986; Lynch Jr. and Srull 1982; Rucker et al. 2014). Rather than engaging effortfully with unfamiliar attributes—such as product specifications—consumers may instead prioritize cues that feel more familiar based on prior experience (Lee 2001; Whittlesea 1993) such as familiar brands and countries of origin.

In experiment 1A, we provided evidence for this prediction. When experiencing perceptual disfluency due to a hard-to-read font, participants chose an option with a familiar brand more often than when the font was easy to read. Consistent with the past work on choice difficulty (Novemsky et al. 2007), we also observed higher rates of deferral under these conditions. The results provide suggestive evidence that choice difficulty may be driving both outcomes. Moreover, these effects occurred above and beyond inattention. When examining the choices of only those participants who correctly recalled the product details (and therefore must have read them), we observed a consistent pattern of results.
EXPERIMENTS 1B–1E: ROBUSTNESS ACROSS DIFFERENT SETTINGS AND DIFFERENT MANIPULATIONS OF PERCEPTUAL DISFLUENCY

Experiments 1B–1E replicated the effect on choice observed in experiment 1A across a range of ecologically valid consumer choice contexts. These included an incentivized decision-making task (1B) and simulated shopping environments that closely mirror the marketplace (1C, 1D, and 1E). In these studies, perceptual disfluency was manipulated in ways that are common in the marketplace, such as background images behind text (1C), low-resolution images containing text (1D), and a small screen sizes (1E). Unlike experiment 1A, these experiments focused on forced-choice scenarios with no possibility to defer. Moreover, in experiments 1D and 1E, participants could make a choice between more than two options.

Through all these experiments, product options were presented either sequentially (1B, 1C, and 1D) or simultaneously on the same screen (1E), with the order of option presentation counterbalanced. In all the experiments, the dependent variable was a choice share of an option featuring a familiar brand name (an intuitively appealing cue). Each experiment contained manipulation checks for perceptual disfluency and brand familiarity. For each set of stimuli, we conducted a pilot study to verify our categorization of cues along the intuitive and analytical dimensions. Detailed analyses of all manipulations for experiments 1B–

In this preregistered study, 1,807 American Prolific participants (Mage ¼ 40.51 years, SD¼14.11; 5.4% female) were randomly assigned to one of two font-based perceptual disfluency conditions previously used in experiment 1A. Participants viewed factual information about two real-world coffee grinders“Cuisinart,” a familiar brand, and “Wirsh,” which had preferable technical specifications (e.g., power, capacity; web appendix B, figures S9 and S10). kids.content: Experiment 1C investigated the impact of perceptual disfluency in a realistic consumer context by manipulating perceptual disfluency through packaging design, a brandcontrolled factor (web appendix A, figure S1). In this preregistered study, 604 American and Canadian Prolific participants (Mage ¼ 43.27 years, SD¼13.34; 51.6% female) viewed two full-screen video clips3 featuring rotating three-dimensional packages of steam irons—“Hamilton Beach” and “Sundu.” Perceptual disfluency was induced by overlaying product information on background images. The fluent condition presented the same information on a plain white background (web appendix B, figures S11 and S12). As predicted, experiment 1C demonstrated that 42.7% of participants viewing packages with information overlaid on background images selected the familiar brand, Hamilton Beach steam iron, whereas only 30.3% of those viewing packages without background images did so (χ2(1) ¼ 9.95, p ¼ .002, Cramer’s V ¼ 0.128).

In this preregistered experiment, 1,202 American and Canadian MTurk participants (Mage ¼ 42.68 years, SD¼13.25; 55.7% female) were asked to choose among four real-world blenders—Hamilton Beach, Kogan, Amzchef, and ICUCINA. Participants in the perceptually fluent condition viewed high-resolution product images, whereas those in the perceptually disfluent condition saw the same images in low resolution. Consistent with prior experiments, one blender featured a familiar brand as an easily comprehended and a salient cue, whereas the remaining three were preferable in product specifications such as capacity, power, and speed settings (web appendix B, figures S13 and S14). As predicted, 41.3% of participants exposed to low-resolution product images chose the familiar brand blender, Hamilton Beach, compared to 32.8% who saw high-resolution images (χ2(1) ¼ 9.17, p ¼ .002, Cramer’s V ¼ 0.09).
Experiment 1E: Multiple Choices and Screen Size

Experiment 1E examined how screen sizes relate to perceptual disfluency. Digital flyers, like those commonly issued by grocery stores, are often distributed in portable document format (PDF), with a fixed layout that is easy to read only when presented at the intended print size. In contrast, when viewing such a flyer on a small screen (like a

mobile device), this type of flyer can present challenges. In this preregistered experiment, 602 Prolific desktop users from the United States and Canada (Mage ¼ 37.75 years, SD¼12.06; 50.4% female) viewed a flyer featuring three real-market blenders—Hamilton Beach, Shardor, and Toycol—either on a tablet-sized mockup (perceptually fluent condition) or a mobile-sized mockup (perceptually disfluent condition; web appendix B, figures S15 and S16). The flyers included real product information and prices as of March 20, 2024. One blender had a more familiar brand name, whereas the other two had preferable technical specifications that needed more careful attention and scrutiny. As predicted, experiment 1E revealed that 56.1% of participants viewing the flyer on a smaller mobile mockup selected the familiar brand, Hamilton Beach blender, compared to 44.2% of participants viewing the flyer on a larger tablet mockup (χ2(1) ¼ 8.61, p ¼ .003, Cramer’s V ¼ 0.12).
EXPERIMENT 2: SERIAL MEDIATION ANALYSIS

Previously, we suggested that perceptual disfluency can lead consumers to interpret difficulty in processing as difficulty deciding (Novemsky et al. 2007), which may in turn shape how they make choices. Rather than engaging with unfamiliar or analytically demanding attributes, consumers

12 MOHSENIN AND MUNZ

experiencing perceptual disfluency may gravitate toward information that feels more certain—such as familiar brands. Experiments 1A–1E provided robust evidence for this effect. Additionally, experiment 1A provided suggestive evidence that the same mechanism may underly this tendency as well as the tendency to defer choosing (Novemsky et al. 2007). Experiment 2 was designed to build on prior findings by more explicitly investigating the psychological mechanism through which perceptual disfluency influences consumer choice.

Specifically, we test a mediation model in which perceptual disfluency increases the subjective difficulty of making a choice or choice difficulty. Additionally, observing that the effects observed in experiment 1 seem to run counter to what might be expected if perceptual fluency encouraged consumers to select options that seem more analytical (Alter et al. 2007), we also examine the subsequent underlying psychological mechanism. In particular, we test whether experiencing perceptual disfluency during decision-making causes consumers to engage in less analytical and more intuitive processing than they otherwise would, directing their attention toward product information that is salient and easy to understand without careful scrutiny. We refer to this change from analytical to intuitive processing as a shift in processing depth.
Method

In this preregistered study, we obtained data for 1,212 American participants using desktop computers (Mage ¼ 42.59 years, SD¼14.11; 54.5% female) from Prolific in exchange for a small payment. All participants who completed the experiment were included in the analysis. Participants were randomly assigned to one of two perceptual disfluency conditions. To manipulate perceptual disfluency, we presented product information with or without a background image to mimic the presentation of real products on Amazon.com (web appendix A; figure S5). Participants were asked to imagine that they needed to buy a new blender. A familiar brand was used as an intuitively appealing cue. As in experiments 1B–1E, for realism, we used actual blender brands. Specifically, Ninja was the familiar brand and Shardor was the less familiar brand. Other information, such as the position of information, blender colors, and body material was kept constant between the two options. We used different background images for each option (figure 4). Participants saw the options presented on sequential screens. Both the order of options and background images were counterbalanced.

We measured the first mediator—choice difficultybefore asking participants to make a choice. Participants were asked to indicate the extent to which they agree with the statements, “I feel unsure about what to choose,” and “It is hard to choose between the two blenders” (1¼not at all; 7¼very much). The two ratings were averaged into an

index of choice difficulty (α ¼0.92). Then, we measured the dependent measure, which was the binary response to the question, “If you were going to buy one of them, which one would you choose?” (Ninja or Shardor). Next, we assessed the second mediator—processing depth—and measured both analytical and intuitive processing separately, each using three-item scales adapted from the literature (Novak and Hoffman 2009; Pocheptsova et al. 2010; Scott and Bruce 1995). Participants reported how they evaluated the options [systematically, carefully, and thoroughly] to measure analytical processing and [intuitively, instinctually, based on gut feelings] to measure intuitive processing using a seven-point scale (1¼not at all, 7¼very much). The three ratings for each processing style were averaged into an index of analytical (α ¼0.93) and intuitive processing (α ¼0.87). Following our preregistration, to form a single processing depth score for each participant, we subtracted intuitive processing from analytical processing. Thus, higher scores would indicate that more analytical processing was used to make the choice, and lower scores would indicate that more intuitive processing was used to make the choice.

Similar to experiment 1A, we included two memory check questions. In addition, participants answered the same question about brand familiarity and difficulty used in the previous studies as manipulation checks.
Results

Manipulation Checks. Both manipulation checks were successful. Participants rated the information presented with a background image (M ¼ 4.62, SD¼2.13) as more difficult to read than without a background image (M ¼ 2.88, SD¼1.81, t(1210) ¼ 15.28, p < .001, d¼0.88). In addition, they were more familiar with the Ninja (vs. Shardor) brand name (MNinja ¼ 4.85, SD¼1.99 vs. MShardor ¼ 2.33, SD¼1.82, t(1211) ¼ 33.53, p < .001, d¼0.96).

Attribute Categorization. We conducted a pilot test to verify our categorization of the attributes along the analytical and intuitive dimensions using the same procedure as experiment 1A (N¼101; Mage ¼ 42.33 years, SD¼14.36; 56.4% female). On the difference score, as predicted, brand was rated as significantly more intuitive than the average of the other features (F(1, 99) ¼ 13.54, p < .001, ηp2 ¼ 0.12). It was also rated significantly more intuitive than each of the attributes individually. Specifically, it was more intuitive and less analytical than blending functions (F(1,99) ¼ 8.73, p ¼ .004, ηp2 ¼ 0.08), the speed setting control (F(1,99) ¼ 7.03, p ¼ .009, ηp2 ¼ 0.07), the blender capacity (F(1,99) ¼ 6.66, p ¼ .011, ηp2 ¼ 0.06), and its power wattage (F(1,99) ¼ 28.91, p < .001, ηp2 ¼ 0.23). Details are provided in web appendix E.

Choice Share. There was no significant brand order effect (p ¼ .96), background image order (p ¼ .50), or their interactions with perceptual disfluency (ps > .17) on choice share. Following our preregistration, combining data on these counterbalancing factors, the result of a chisquare test revealed the predicted effect of perceptual disfluency (χ2(1) ¼ 38.45, p < .001, Cramer’s V ¼ 0.178). Among participants who saw product information on a white background, 35.4% chose the Ninja blender, whereas 53.1% of those who saw the information with a background image did so, replicating the previous experiments.

Serial Mediation (Choice Difficulty and Processing Depth). We proposed that perceptual disfluency would lead to higher difficulty in making a choice that lowers the depth of processing. As we predicted, we found that participants who experienced perceptual disfluency felt more indecision (Mbackground_image ¼ 4.35, SD¼1.83;
Mno_background 3.77, SD 1.91, t(1210) 5.33, p < .001, d¼0.31) and evaluated the options less deeply

(Mbackground_image ¼ 0.6, SD¼2.06; Mno_background ¼ 0.99, SD¼2.04, t(1210) ¼ 3.29, p ¼ .001, d¼0.19). More details are provided in web appendix D. As depicted in figure 5, we employed a serial mediation model using PROCESS model 6 with 10,000 iterations (Preacher and Hayes 2004) with perceptual disfluency as the independent variable, choice difficulty as the first mediator, processing depth as the second mediator, and choice share of an option with familiar brand name as the dependent variable. This analysis indicated significant serial mediation (bindirect_effect ¼ 0.055, standard error [SE] ¼0.014, 95% CI [0.031, 0.086]), indicating the data are consistent with our proposal that perceptual disfluency increases the feeling of indecision, which decreases the depth of processing and increases the reliance on a familiar brand name to make the choice.

Memory Checks. Participants in the perceptually fluent condition answered correctly more often than participants in the perceptually disfluent condition for both memory items (blender power in wattage: Pwhite_background ¼ 85.3% vs. Pbackground_image ¼ 72.1% correct; χ2(1) ¼ 31.58, p < .001; number of blending functions: Pwhite_background ¼ 87.8% vs. Pbackground_image ¼ 78.1% correct; χ2(1) ¼ 20.06, p <.001).

As in experiment 1A, we explored whether the effect persisted among the subset of participants who recalled the details correctly. Among the 71.4% of participants who responded correctly to all memory-check questions (N¼865/1,212), the pattern of results did not change. That is, participants chose the Ninja blender at a higher rate (Pbackground_image ¼ 40.3%, N ¼153/380 vs. Pwhite_background ¼ 26.0%, N¼126/485) when it felt hard to read the information (χ2(1) ¼ 19.89, p < .001, Cramer’s V ¼ 0.152). This is further evidence that when examining only participants who actually read the information, the effect persists.

We also tested for serial mediation among this subset of participants. The result of a bootstrapping technique (model 6; Preacher and Hayes 2004) with 10,000 iterations revealed that the differences between choice difficulty and processing depth mediated the effect of disfluency on the choice share (bindirect_effect ¼ 0.06, SE ¼0.019, 95% CI [0.030, 0.102]).
Discussion
The results of experiment 2 replicate and extend the patterns observed in experiments 1A–1E. They provide evidence for the underlying mechanism by directly testing the

mediating roles of choice difficulty and processing depth. Specifically, we find that perceptual disfluency increases the subjective difficulty of making a decision. This increased choice difficulty and in turn shifts consumers away from analytical processing and toward more intuitive information processing—a shift in processing depth that ultimately guides them toward choices that feel more familiar or easier to comprehend, such as selecting a wellknown brand. Interestingly, although prior work suggests that perceptual disfluency can encourage more analytical processing (Alter et al. 2007), our results show that perceptual disfluency in a consumer choice context may instead push individuals away from analytical thinking.

These findings are further supported by two supplementary studies reported in web appendix D. In experiment W1, participants selected choice justifications from a list containing both technical (analytical) and intuitively appealing cues. In experiment W2, we replicated this finding using a self-report measure. Participants rated the extent to which they relied on intuitive versus analytical processing using scales parallel to those in this experiment, and results again confirmed the mediating role of processing depth. Together, these studies bolster the evidence that perceptual disfluency increases intuitive and reduces analytical processing, pushing consumers to make choices that are guided more by intuitively appealing product information.

In experiment 2, we found that perceptual disfluency increases feelings of indecision and reduces processing

Experiment 3 builds on this by testing whether lay theories of decision speed (i.e., beliefs about whether fast or slow decisions lead to better outcomes) moderate this effect. Prior work shows that manipulating beliefs about quick versus slow decisions (Inbar, Botti, and Hanko 2011) influences the subjective experience of haste. Meanwhile, a large body of research on time-pressure shows that when decision time is constrained, individuals shift toward simpler decision strategies (Payne, Bettman, and Johnson 1988) and rely on fewer attributes (Svenson, Edland, and Karlsson 1985). Accordingly, if perceptual disfluency already increases reliance on salient cues like familiar brands (as shown in experiments 1A–1E and 2), then endorsing a decision speed lay theory favoring a quick decision should amplify this tendency.

Participants were told they needed to buy a new printer, and they found two options that met their needs. Similar to previous experiments, one of the printers was preferable on numerical cues (i.e., larger paper tray size and printing output, higher printing speed, voice printing feature, and more connectivity options). In contrast, the other printer had a more familiar brand name. For realism, in this study, we used actual printer brands: EPSON as a familiar brand and PANTUM as a less familiar brand. Other information, such as price and the printer color, was held constant between the two options. All participants saw both options presented sequentially on different screens. The presentation order was counterbalanced.
Method

Six hundred two American and Canadian online panel participants who completed the study on a desktop computer (Mage ¼ 41.55 years, SD ¼12.73; 53.5% female) and were recruited through MTurk took part in this preregistered experiment in exchange for payment. This study used a 2 (perceptual disfluency: low [no background image] vs. high [a background image]) by 2 (decision speed lay theory: fast is accurate vs. slow is accurate) between-participants design. The dependent variable was the proportion of participants choosing the printer with a more familiar brand name. All participants who completed the experiment were included in the analysis.

Checking for brand familiarity and perceptual disfluency manipulations, we asked the same questions as in the previous experiments. As a check of our processing speed manipulation, we used both subjective and objective measurements, following the existing literature. Regarding the subjective check (Dhar and Nowlis 1999), participants were requested to answer two questions: (1) “How much time pressure did you feel when making your choice?” (1¼no pressure, 7 ¼very much pressure), and (2) “How fast did you need to make your decisions?” (1 not at all fast, 7¼very fast). To check the objective measurement, we measured the time spent on the pages to read the information (Allard et al. 2019).

Before participants made their choice, they were randomly assigned to one of the four conditions. We manipulated perceptual disfluency, similar to experiment 2 by presenting the two printers to half of the participants with the textual information presented over a background image,4 whereas the others saw the same information about the same printers without a photo background (web appendix B, figures S17 and S18).
Results

Manipulation Checks. Participants rated the information presented with a background image as more difficult to read (M ¼ 4.03, SD¼1.91) than without a background image (M ¼ 2.81, SD¼1.75, t(600) ¼ 8.15 p < .001, d¼0.66). The manipulation of brand familiarity was also successful. A paired-sample t-test on brand familiarity revealed more familiarity with EPSON (M ¼5.33, SD¼1.92) than PANTUM (M ¼ 1.45, SD¼0.98, t(601) ¼ 43.39, p < .001, d ¼1.77). Furthermore, participants took longer to reach a decision in the slow-is-accurate lay theory condition (M ¼45.69, SD¼38.03) compared with the fastis-accurate lay theory condition (M ¼15.78, SD¼13.64; t (600) ¼ 12.86, p < .001, d¼1.05). Moreover, participants felt more time pressure (α¼0.89) in the fast-is-accurate condition (Mfast-is-accurate ¼ 5.33, SD¼1.32; Mslow-is-accurate ¼ 2.17, SD¼1.16; t(600) ¼ 31.19, p < .001, d¼2.54).

We manipulated decision speed lay theories following past research (Allard, Hardisty, and Griffin 2019; Inbar et al. 2011; web appendix C). Half of the participants saw information reporting that “fast is accurate” for making decisions. They read that psychological research showed that “people often make better choices when they decide quickly and effortlessly. A quick response often leads to making the best choice possible.” The text concluded with instructions to “quickly consider the options and immediately make your selection.” This manipulation creates a sense of time pressure in the “fast-is-accurate” condition

Participants in the perceptually fluent condition answered correctly more often than participants in the perceptually disfluent condition for both memory check items, but the result was NS for the latter one (voiceactivated printer: Pfluent ¼ 89.3% vs. Pdisfluent ¼ 74.6% correct; χ2(1) ¼ 21.96, p < .001; EPSON printer’s tray capacity: Pfluent ¼ 85.9% vs. Pdisfluent ¼ 84.5% correct; χ2(1) < 1, p ¼ .625).

Results were consistent, showing effects of perceptual disfluency (Wald χ2(1) ¼ 15.88, p < .001, OR¼0.449; 95% CI [0.303, 0.666]) and decision speed lay theory (Wald χ2(1) ¼ 8.96, p ¼ .003, OR¼2.47; 95% CI [1.37, 4.46]) and an interaction effect (Wald χ2(1) ¼ 4.24, p ¼ .039, OR¼0.44; 95% CI [0.20, 0.96]). Although 49.5% of participants chose the EPSON printer in the slow-is-accurate lay theory condition when it was hard to process the information, only 40% did so when it was easy to process the information (χ2(1) ¼ 2.15, p ¼ .14, Cramer’s V 0.10). The effect was strengthened when participants processed the information quickly and effortlessly (70.8% vs. 41.8%; χ2(1) ¼ 16.66, p < .001, Cramer’s V ¼ 0.29).
Discussion

In this experiment, we manipulated both decision speed lay theory and perceptual disfluency, predicting an interaction between the two. The results supported the conclusion that believing that making a fast decision can lead to a better outcome magnifies the effect of perceptual disfluency on consumer choice. If making a fast decision increases reliance on important attributes (Payne et al. 1988; Svenson et al. 1985), this experiment provides suggestive evidence that perceptual disfluency makes intuitively appealing product cues like brands more important. Moreover, these effects occurred above and beyond inattention, replicating previous findings in experiments 1A and 2.
EXPERIMENT 4: THE ROLE OF CHOICE DIFFICULTY

Consumers may misinterpret perceptual disfluency as choice difficulty—a sense of indecision (experiment 2; Novemsky et al. 2007). This is a metacognitive process, as it requires consumers to think about their own thoughts or feelings to make this (mistaken) inference. To test if the effects we have observed are indeed due to a metacognitive process, in this experiment, we employed an attributional discounting paradigm, following existing research (Lee and Shavitt 2009; Novemsky et al. 2007; Pocheptsova et al. 2010; Sela and Berger 2012; Walter et al. 2020). In such a paradigm, if the experimenter makes a participant aware

that the feeling of perceptual disfluency is due to the manipulation (rather than choice difficulty), the effects on choices should be diminished. In other words, if perceptual disfluency no longer leads to an inference of choice difficulty, the consequences for choices should also disappear.
Method

In this preregistered experiment, we obtained data for 602 British participants from Prolific who completed the study in exchange for a small payment (Mage ¼ 45.68 years, SD¼13.39; 58% female). All participants were included in the analysis. We randomly assigned participants to one of three conditions: perceptually fluent (easyto-read font), perceptually disfluent (hard-to-read font), and attribution (difficult-to-read font with source attribution). We manipulated perceptual disfluency using the same fonts as in experiments 1A and 1B. To manipulate the source attribution, before completing the decision task, some participants were informed that the font will make reading the text harder. Others were not told this information (Lee and Shavitt 2009; Novemsky et al. 2007; Pocheptsova et al. 2010; Sela and Berger 2012; Walter et al. 2020). This additional information was intended to prompt participants to (correctly) attribute the experienced difficulty to the font rather than difficulty choosing, which should attenuate the impact of perceptual disfluency on
choice (Alter and Oppenheimer 2009; Schwarz et al. 1991).

The options were toasters, and they were similar to those in the previous studies. One option had preferable numerical specifications, and the other option featured an intuitively appealing cue, a recommendation from Amazon (web appendix B; figures S19 and S20). Among the list of product features, the recommendation was mentioned last. Our aim in doing so was to minimize the likelihood that a participant would choose based on the recommendation without reading the remaining details. Participants saw information about both alternatives simultaneously, with the order of the toasters counterbalanced.

The dependent measure was the binary response to the question, “If you were going to buy one of them, which one would you choose?” (toaster A or toaster B). Near the end, we included two memory check questions, “What was the number of toast shade settings of the recommended toaster? (Five toast shade settings or Seven toast shade settings)” and “Which toaster had a higher power (1100watts)? (Toaster A or Toaster B)” and asked participants to rate their experience of difficulty in reading information as a manipulation check of font as in the previous experiments.

who responded correctly to both memory questions, reasoning that participants must first read the information in order to remember it. The same relative pattern of results emerged as presented earlier. Specifically, 37.2% (N¼48/ 129) of participants who were not aware of the source of perceptual disfluency chose the recommended toaster in the perceptually disfluent condition, compared to 20.7% (N¼21/135) in the perceptually fluent condition (χ2(1) ¼ 8.73, p ¼ .003, Cramer’s V ¼ 0.182) and 22.5% (N¼31/ 138) when there was an attribution warning present (χ2(1) ¼ 6.96, p ¼ .008, Cramer’s V ¼ 0.161). There was no significant difference between the perceptually fluent and the attribution conditions on the effect of perceptual disfluency on the recommended toaster choice share (p ¼ .729). Thus, the overall results are unlikely to be entirely explained by differences in attention.
Discussion

Experiment 4 provided further evidence that the effect may be metacognitive in nature. Specifically, people tend to misattribute feelings of perceptual disfluency to the choice task, which reinforces feelings of indecision. Thus, they tend to rely on easily comprehended and intuitively appealing cues during consumer choices. The results demonstrate that when they do not attribute the feelings of difficulty to the choice task, this effect disappears.

Alternatively, people may have paid greater attention when given a warning about the font. However, we observed a consistent pattern among participants with equal recall (which indicates attention). This suggests that the effect is at least partially metacognitive, requiring an inference about the source of difficulty for the effect to come about.
EXPERIMENT 5—THE ROLE OF CHOOSING

Misattributing perceptual disfluency as difficulty choosing (choice difficulty) should only occur when consumers are making choices. Thus, we predict that effects of perceptual disfluency should occur for consumer choices but not for a related task that does not involve choosing. This could be one potential explanation to reconcile the effects we have observed with those in the existing literature. Specifically, some studies have found that disfluency encourages more analytical processing (Alter et al. 2007; Song and Schwarz 2008) or null effects (Meyer et al. 2015; Thompson et al. 2013). These studies, however, examined situations that mostly involved solving logical puzzles. In contrast, for consumer choices, our results consistently show that perceptual disfluency reduces processing depth, shifting individuals toward more intuitive and less analytical processing. This experiment examines if making choices is a critical element.

Method

In this preregistered experiment, 604 British participants (Mage ¼ 41.12 years, SD¼12.88; 58.9% female) from Prolific were paid a small sum for completing this experiment. All participants were included in the analysis. The stimuli were identical to those used in experiment 4, which involved the attribute categorization task. This study followed a 2 (perceptual disfluency: hard-to-read font vs. easy-to-read font) by 2 (task: choice vs. no choice) between-participants design. To manipulate perceptual disfluency, we used the same fonts as in the previous experiments (1A, 1B, and 4). To manipulate the task, we asked participants either to make a choice or to identify the option with better specifications (i.e., not making a choice). That is, half of the participants were asked to choose between the options to purchase: “If you were going to choose between them, which one would you like to buy?” The others in the no-choice condition were asked, “Which toaster in this survey had better specs?” Whereas one of the toasters was preferable based on technical specifications (i.e., it had higher power, more toast-shade settings, and wider slot size), the other toaster was recommended (Amazon’s choice), an intuitively appealing cue that is easier to comprehend. We counterbalanced the order of toasters. The dependent measure was the choice of the recommended toaster from a binary choice set, and it was asked on the same page of the products information.

After responding to the dependent measure, participants were asked to rate the difficulty of processing information as a manipulation check of perceptual disfluency and another question about the objectivity of the task, which we do not discuss further.
Results

Manipulation Check. Participants rated the information presented in the hard-to-read font (M 4.46, SD 2.08) as more difficult to read than the easy-to-read font (M 1.73, SD¼1.23, t(602) ¼ 19.64 p < .001, d¼1.60).

Choice Share. Following our preregistration, the result showed that there was no significant order effect of the recommended toaster (p ¼ .28), and we collapsed across the counterbalancing factor. As predicted and depicted in figure 8, we found a significant interaction of perceptual disfluency and task on a choice share of the recommended toaster (Wald χ2(1) ¼ 4.71, p ¼ .030, OR ¼2.38, 95% CI [1.09, 5.23]). Follow-up analyses within each task revealed that when people were asked to choose their preferred option to purchase, 19.9% of participants who saw the easy-to-read font chose the recommended toaster, whereas 40.4% of those who saw the hard-to-read font did so (χ2(1) ¼ 15.12, p < .001, Cramer’s V ¼ 0.224). This effect was not observed when participants were asked to identify the option with better specs (p ¼ .123), as 15.9% of participants who saw the hard-to-read font found the recommended toaster to have better specs and 9.9% of those who saw the easy-to-read font did so.
Discussion

Perceptual disfluency is highly task dependent (Schwarz 2004), and this experiment further demonstrates that divergent results can arise from variations in the task. In a consumer choice context, these findings reveal that individuals tend to rely more on easy-to-comprehend cues to make

Conversely, for a task in which misinterpreting perceptual disfluency as choice difficulty is much less likely, people do not seem to be affected by it. This aligns with previous research indicating that perceptual disfluency may not affect certain tasks (e.g., Meyer et al. 2015; Thompson et al. 2013).

Alternatively, it may be that the tasks examined here also differed in the extent to which they offered a loweffort solution. That is, relying on a familiar brand does not seem like an effective strategy to identify a product with the best specifications, but it could be one that produces a satisfactory choice. We discuss this idea further in the General Discussion.
GENERAL DISCUSSION

Eleven experiments have highlighted how perceptual disfluency leads decision-makers to rely more readily on familiar brands, trusted countries of origin, or recommendations when choosing, processing information more intuitively and less analytically than otherwise. The results differ from earlier findings (Alter et al. 2007; Meyer et al. 2015) but only occur when decision-makers mistake perceptual disfluency for indecision.

Experiments 1A–1E established the robustness of the effect across multiple sources of perceptual disfluency: a difficult-to-read font (1A and 1B), a background image behind text (1C), a low-resolution image containing text (1D), and a small screen size (1E). In each case,

participants were more likely to choose the option from a familiar brand when the choice materials were perceptually disfluent, even when choices were incentive compatible (1B) and when presented with multiple options (1D and 1E). Experiment 2 demonstrated how perceptual disfluency increased choice difficulty and decreased processing depth, leading to the effect on choice. Experiment 3 demonstrated that the effects of perceptual disfluency on consumer choices are amplified when fast decision-making lay theories are applied. Experiment 4 tested the metacognitive inference that decision-makers may misattribute perceptual disfluency for choice difficulty (Novemsky et al. 2007). Consistent with this account, the effect of perceptual disfluency was attenuated when participants were warned that the difficulty stemmed from the font. Finally, experiment 5 demonstrated that this misattribution emerges only in tasks that involve choosing. When participants were instead asked to identify the option with the “best specs,” perceptual disfluency had no effect, consistent with prior null results in other non-choice contexts (Meyer et al. 2015).

These findings are important for two key reasons. First, they differ from prior work suggesting that perceptual disfluency promotes analytical thinking (Alter et al. 2007) or has no effect on reasoning (Meyer et al. 2015). In contrast, we show that when choosing between products, perceptual disfluency shifts consideration away from analytical or numeric information and toward intuitively appealing information such as familiar brands. Unlike prior studies that involved tasks in which disfluency might arouse suspicion, our results suggest that for consumer choices, perceptual disfluency can be misinterpreted as difficulty

choosing, prompting reliance on intuitively appealing product information, extending prior work on this topic (Novemsky et al. 2007). Second, the findings offer practical implications. When marketing materials reduce perceptual fluency, consumers are more likely to favor familiar options. This insight can guide marketers and designers seeking to shape consumer preferences.
Beyond Deferral and Compromise

Our research builds on past results showing that consumers can mistake perceptual disfluency for choice difficulty—a feeling of indecision experienced when considering trade-offs between options—leading them to defer choosing or to select a compromise option (Novemsky et al. 2007). From this perspective, consumers can avoid an apparently hard trade-off by not choosing or by selecting a compromise option. In contrast, we have studied choices in which trade-offs are unavoidable, and it is as yet unclear how disfluency would affect choices in such situations. We find that, in addition to deferral and compromise, consumers who mistake perceptual disfluency for choice difficulty exhibit a tendency to favor options with familiar brands, from preferred countries of origin, or that are recommended.
Additionally, we provide new evidence that consumers mistake perceptual disfluency for choice difficulty in this context. This includes mediation evidence from experiment

list items.content: 2 (directly measuring the inference) and moderation evidence from experiments 4 and 5 (replicating attributional discounting with a novel outcome and showing that the effect disappears in non-choice tasks).

kids.content: Furthermore, in experiments 2, W1, and W2, we directly tested whether perceptual disfluency affects processing depth, finding that it increases intuitive and decreases analytical processing, a novel finding in this research and one that stands in contrast to some previous results (Alter et al. 2007). Notably, this finding does not directly follow from the past work (Novemsky et al. 2007), as identifying a compromise option generally requires analytical effort (Dhar, Nowlis, and Sherman 2000). We discuss this idea further in the next section.

We also consider why there may be inconsistent effects of perceptual disfluency. One explanation could be that consumers’ responses to perceptual disfluency may vary depending on the task. Here, we focused on consumer choices, whereas many previous studies examined math and logic-based tasks. In consumer decisions, disfluency might signal that the choice is difficult, a factor that does not apply in those settings (experiment 5).
When Does Choice Difficulty Affect Processing?

Choice difficulty can lead consumers to defer choosing until later or to select a compromise option (Novemsky et al. 2007). It can also lead them to choose on the basis of easily noticed and easily understood information like

brands or recommendations (this article). On the one hand, deferring choice or choosing based on a familiar brand or recommendation can be an effective way to reduce effort, reducing the need for careful analytical information processing. On the other hand, there is some evidence that selecting a compromise option (Novemsky et al. 2007) is the result of a more analytical process (Dhar et al. 2000). This raises a question as to when choice difficulty affects processing in the manner we document.

One possible answer to the question may be related to the set of available options. Choice sets that permit deferral or contain a familiar brand, preferred country of origin, or recommended alternative—conditions examined here—may encourage more intuitive and less analytical responses. In a sense, they offer an “easy out,” and some consumers may be tempted by it when choosing feels difficult due to perceptual disfluency. In contrast, choice sets that lack such low-effort options could compel deeper processing. In such cases, some past research has suggested that perceptual disfluency could enhance the tendency to process analytically (Alter et al. 2007; Song and Schwarz 2008), whereas other research has suggested that it has no effect (Meyer et al. 2015; Thompson et al. 2013).

Notably, several theories on dual information processing systems described earlier have proposed similar ideas. For instance, some theories describe a “least effort principle” or a “law of least effort,” which asserts that people try to make decisions efficiently as possible (Chaiken and Ledgerwood 2012; Kahneman 2011). This aligns with the idea that people will try to save effort when they can, acting as “cognitive misers” (Cacioppo et al. 1986).

The results of experiment 5 could also be interpreted through this lens. We have stated how a mistaken inference about choice difficulty should only occur when someone experiencing perceptual disfluency engages in decisionmaking. However, in experiment 5, the two tasks also differed in the extent to which they offered a low-effort solution. That is, identifying the option with the best specifications may require analytical processing. Future research could investigate this question more directly.
Metacognitive versus Cognitive Process

Consumers can mistake perceptual disfluency for choice difficulty, a feeling of indecision (Novemsky et al. 2007). This is a metacognitive process, as it requires consumers to think about their own thoughts or feelings to make this (mistaken) inference. Several of the results presented here align with this interpretation. In experiment 2, we tested a serial mediation model that demonstrated that consumers do, in fact, make this inference. In experiment 4, we employed an attributional discounting paradigm, following past literature (Fang et al. 2007; Novemsky et al. 2007; Pocheptsova et al. 2010; Schwarz et al. 1991; Sela and Berger 2012; Walter et al. 2020). For such tasks, when

people are explicitly warned that the difficulty they are experiencing is caused by something irrelevant (e.g., poor font choice), the negative effects on their evaluations are reduced or even eliminated, and we observed this result. Additionally, in experiment 5, we reasoned that misinterpreting perceptual disfluency as decision difficulty should only occur in contexts involving choice. Accordingly, when participants completed a similar task that required identifying the option with the “best specs” rather than making a choice, we did not observe the same pattern of results. Taken together, these results support the possibility that the results are driven by a metacognitive process.

Alternatively, disfluency may influence consumers’ decisions through a process that does not involve metacognition. One such cognitive process could occur if, when noticing poor fonts or distracting background images, consumers recognize that processing product information will be difficult and they read less of it as a result. There is evidence for an effect of perceptual disfluency above and beyond this inattention account. For instance, in experiment 1B, choice outcomes were incentive compatible. This type of design motivates participants to pay more careful attention than otherwise, and the results were consistent. Furthermore, in experiment 3, we asked some participants to make slow and careful decisions, but differences between perceptually fluent and disfluent presentations were still observed. Additionally, in several of the experiments, we conducted additional analyses on a subset of participants who correctly recalled the product details. If recall is a proxy for attention, this analysis better controls attention between the conditions. In all the experiments with this analysis (experiments 1A, 2, 3, 4, W1, and W2), we still observed a significant effect.
Managerial and Practical Implications

It is not hard to find examples of perceptual disfluency in the marketplace, and this research highlights important consequences for consumer decision-making. Specifically, we have investigated several instances of perceptual disfluency that were inspired by situations commonly experienced by consumers.

For instance, a common design element in flyers or other print media is to present descriptive text on top of an image (web appendix A, figure S5). Although there may be aesthetic benefits, this practice can lead to perceptual disfluency when the background image and the text do not have enough contrast with each other (Reber et al. 1998; Thompson and Ince 2013). As shown in experiments 1C, 2,

list items.content: 3, and W2, such elements can enhance consumers’ tendency to accept recommendations or lead them to choose familiar brands more often. An unscrupulous retailer could leverage this knowledge to subtly impact shoppers’ decisions. Thus, both consumers and policymakers should be cognizant of the potential for such effects.

kids.content: Another context in which perceptual disfluency is common relates to screen sizes. Grocers and other retailers often produce flyers to advertise promotional prices (web appendix A, figure S6). Frequently, these flyers are in PDF for distribution online. This format was developed before the ubiquity of mobile phones, and it uses a fixed, flat layout. In other words, it does not adapt based on the size of the screen. Consumers reading these flyers on mobile devices (e.g., when shopping in stores) may struggle to read product information, leading to disfluency, and this can disproportionately favor well-established brands (experiment 1E).

This research also considers a factor that brands can control—packaging design (experiment 1C). As a key marketing tool, effective packaging differentiates products from competitors on the shelves. However, when packaging is difficult to read—due to unclear labels, small text, or background images—it can impact the consumer decisionmaking process. Our research shows that such design issues may lead to perceptual disfluency, particularly affecting less-established brands in the market. Along these lines, the research also highlights how marketing managers for less-established brands should critically focus on ensuring fluency in their marketing materials. That is, we do not advocate for well-established brands to create disfluency in hopes of gaining market share. Instead, we hope to encourage less-established brands to improve the fluency in their marketing materials to avoid any unnecessary disadvantage. This provides them with benefits while additionally improving the user experience for the customer.
Limitations

We have highlighted how the consumer response to perceptual disfluency when making a choice may not be the same as in other circumstances. Accordingly, our results are limited to choices made in consumer settings. Moreover, we have investigated circumstances in which the types of information of interest (intuitively appealing information like brands and recommends and more analytical information like numerical product specifications) favored different choice outcomes. One should proceed with caution in applying our findings to situations that do not involve this dynamic. Additionally, we have studied a particular form of disfluency—perceptual disfluency. Although some have argued that the forms of fluency can often be similar (Alter and Oppenheimer 2009), our results cannot directly speak to any other form of fluency.
Conclusion

When consumers mistake perceptual disfluency for indecision, they become more likely to rely on familiar brands, trusted countries of origin, or recommendations—favoring intuitive over analytical processing. These findings differ
